context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the draft climate action plan prospectus for my case study city, and I’m a little stuck on choosing two strong policy components. I think I understand the basic structure, but I’m not sure how to make the recommendations specific enough or connect them to environmental justice. Could you help me narrow that down?
exchanges.tutor: Great starting point! Before we dig into the components, tell me: what city are you working with, and what were the main vulnerabilities or themes that came out of your SWOT analysis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the assignment and needs to get started. Rather than scaffolding or giving examples, I should ask them to share what they already have - their case study city and SWOT analysis findings - so we can work from their own research. This keeps them in the driver's seat and helps me understand where they actually are before providing any guidance.

exchanges.student: I’m using Mumbai. From my SWOT, I think the big issues were flooding, extreme heat, informal settlements being more exposed, and probably infrastructure overload too. I’m not totally sure which of those should turn into the two components though.
exchanges.tutor: You've got solid material there. Think about this: the assignment asks you to consider overlaps between components AND environmental justice implications. Looking at your list - flooding, extreme heat, informal settlements, infrastructure overload - which of those issues do you think most directly affects vulnerable communities, and do any of them connect to each other in interesting ways?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good raw material from their SWOT. Rather than helping them choose, I should prompt them to think about which issues are most interconnected and have strong EJ implications - which is exactly what the assignment asks for. This guides them toward making their own reasoned choice without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I think flooding and heat both hit vulnerable communities the most, especially people in informal settlements. Infrastructure overload seems connected too, but I’m having trouble turning that into a policy instead of just a problem. Is it better if I pick flooding and heat as the two components?
exchanges.tutor: Flooding and heat are reasonable choices, but don't dismiss infrastructure so quickly. The assignment asks you to identify an *opportunity* for action, not just a problem. What could be done about infrastructure overload in Mumbai, and could that connect to either flooding or heat in a meaningful way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress in their reasoning. Their instinct about flooding and heat is defensible, but I want to push them to think more critically rather than just validating their choice. The infrastructure point is actually interesting and worth exploring - it could strengthen either component. I should ask them to think harder about what 'infrastructure overload' could mean as a policy opportunity rather than just a problem, since that kind of thinking is exactly what the assignment's 'Opportunity' section requires.

exchanges.student: Maybe infrastructure overload could mean upgrading drainage, transit, or electricity systems? I guess drainage connects to flooding pretty directly, and electricity connects to heat if more people need cooling. I’m still kind of fuzzy on whether that should be its own component or part of a flooding/heat policy.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction. Look back at the assignment structure - there's a specific section called 'Interactions' in each component. What do you think that section is there for, and how might that change your decision about whether infrastructure needs its own component?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good connection between infrastructure and the two main issues. Rather than resolving the 'own component vs. part of another' question for them, I should point them to the assignment's 'Interactions' section, which is designed exactly for capturing these synergies. This keeps them thinking and helps them see the structure of the assignment more clearly.

exchanges.student: Oh, I think that means infrastructure doesn’t have to be its own component if it supports the other two. So maybe I could do one component on flood resilience and one on extreme heat, and then mention drainage and grid upgrades in the interactions section. Is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: That structure makes sense. Now here's the real challenge you identified at the start - specificity. Pick one of your two components and tell me: what exactly is the *current situation* in Mumbai for that issue, and what specific *opportunity* for action do you see? Try to be as concrete as possible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a good structural decision on their own. Rather than validating or critiquing the breadth question abstractly, I should push them toward specificity - which is exactly what they said they were struggling with at the start. Now is a good moment to transition from planning to actually developing the content of one component, starting with the Context and Opportunity sections.

exchanges.student: I’ll try flooding. The current situation is that Mumbai gets severe monsoon flooding, and low-income communities, especially in informal settlements, are more exposed because of poor drainage and housing conditions. The opportunity might be investing in neighborhood-scale drainage upgrades and maybe nature-based flood control, but I’m not sure if that’s specific enough yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good start - you've got the bones of a Context and an Opportunity. Now push yourself: what does 'neighborhood-scale drainage upgrades' actually look like in Mumbai specifically? And what nature-based solutions might make sense given Mumbai's geography? Getting concrete on those details is what will make your Policy Recommendation strong.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft of the Context and Opportunity sections. This is good progress. Rather than telling them whether it's specific enough, I should push them to interrogate their own specificity by asking what 'neighborhood-scale' and 'nature-based' actually mean in Mumbai's context. This moves them toward the Policy Recommendation section, which is the core 200-300 word deliverable.

exchanges.student: I think it could look like stormwater drain expansion in flood-prone wards, better maintenance so drains aren’t blocked, and maybe restoring mangroves or wetlands since Mumbai is coastal. I’m guessing that would reduce flood risk, but I’m not sure how detailed I need to be for a 200–300 word recommendation.
exchanges.tutor: You have enough to start writing. The 200-300 word limit will actually guide you on depth - you'll feel quickly whether you're being too vague or too detailed. Try drafting your Policy Recommendation section now using the ideas you just laid out, then we can look at what's working.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good specific ideas now - drain expansion, maintenance, mangrove/wetland restoration. These are concrete and Mumbai-relevant. Rather than answering the word count question abstractly, I should redirect them to just try writing the recommendation. They have enough material. The best way to find out if it's detailed enough is to attempt a draft. This also follows the rule about asking students to integrate discussion into written content after several exchanges on the same idea.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll take a shot, but I want help checking if it sounds too generic. I’m thinking of recommending targeted drainage upgrades in flood-prone informal settlements, regular drain clearing, and mangrove restoration to reduce coastal flooding.
exchanges.tutor: That's still a plan, not a draft. Go ahead and write the actual 200-300 words - full sentences, your own voice. Once you have something written down, we can talk about whether it's specific enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is describing their plan rather than actually writing the draft. I should push them to actually write it rather than continuing to plan. They have all the ingredients and have been circling this for several exchanges. I should be direct about this without being harsh.

exchanges.student: Got it. I’m a little nervous about writing a full draft from scratch, but I’ll try. Could you first give me a super short outline for the recommendation so I don’t make it too messy?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the nerves, but providing an outline would be doing part of the work for you - and you actually already have one. Look back at the assignment structure (Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions) and our conversation. That's your roadmap. Give it a shot - a messy first draft is still a draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do preparatory work for them, which crosses the line into completing tasks for them. The assignment structure is already clearly laid out, and we've discussed the content thoroughly. I should decline but redirect them to what they already have - the assignment's own structure and our conversation - as their guide.

exchanges.student: Okay, fair. I’ll write a rough version for the flooding policy recommendation now, and then I’d like help tightening it after.
exchanges.tutor: Great - go for it. When you come back with your draft, I'll give you honest feedback on whether it's specific and well-structured, but the revising and rewriting will be yours to do. Looking forward to seeing what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has committed to writing a draft, which is exactly where we want them. I should encourage them briefly and set clear expectations for what we'll do when they return with their draft - evaluate it critically and help them improve it through questions, not by editing for them. This is a good moment to remind them that I won't be polishing their language.
